title: uasymsg
text: uasymsg Send an asynchronous message to a local Uniface application. long uasymsg (long pid , char* destkey , char* message ); Parameters pid Process Id of the Uniface process to which the message must be sent. destkey Obsolete. Always use the value zero. message Message to send. The message must not be longer than 249 bytes. Return Values Table 1. Values returned by uasymsg Value Meaning 1 The message was successfully sent. -1 The call failed. The message was not sent. Use Unix platforms only (except Tru64 Unix), in library ulib Description uasymsg sends a string message to the Uniface process identified by pid . That application must be listening to asynchronous messages, that is, it must have called uasync or be using the assignment setting $IPC_ASYNC . Strictly speaking, uasymsg does not send the message, it just puts it into an IPC message queue, the key of that queue being equal to the pid of the receiving process. It does not check whether that process or queue actually exists. If the pid is wrong, or the process does not listen for asynchronous messages, uasymsg will create the queue and write the message to it, and that queue will just sit there, never to be read or removed. To enable sending a message without having to write 3GL code, Uniface provides the program pidfmess , which has the same functionality as uasymsg . The following example sends a message to a Uniface application with process Id 12345: Explain this code uasymsg(12345, 0, "Here's a message for process id 12345"); Copy Related Links uasync Initialize the Uniface application to receive asynchronous messages. unoasync Disable the handling of asynchronous messages by the application.
